description: Transactional sale of physical goods to consumers, online or in-store. Levers are demand generation (acquisition, conversion), unit economics (AOV, margin), retention (repeat purchase), and inventory efficiency.

metrics.name: Average Order Value (AOV)
metrics.grain: Order grain. If revenue lives on line items, SUM the line revenue and divide by COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) — never average line totals.
anti_patterns: AVG(order_items.line_total) computes average LINE value, not order value.
After joining orders to items, COUNT(*) counts lines, not orders — dividing revenue by it understates AOV.
metrics.sane_range: USD, strictly positive; typically tens to low-hundreds for DTC.

metrics.name: Cart-to-Order Conversion Rate
metrics.definition: Share of shopping carts (or sessions) that result in a placed/paid order.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT converted_cart_id) / COUNT(DISTINCT cart_id), where a cart is 'converted' iff an order references it (cart_id / session_id / anon_id).
metrics.grain: Cart grain. Carts and orders are each per session/visitor; relate them at the cart key. A conversion rate is bounded 0..1 BY CONSTRUCTION.
anti_patterns: COUNT(orders) / COUNT(carts) as raw totals → can exceed 1 (the classic >1 bug). If you get >1, the join grain is wrong, not the data.
Counting order_items instead of distinct orders fans out the numerator.
A chasm join (carts ⋈ orders both many-side of a session) multiplies counts — pre-aggregate each side to the cart key first.

metrics.name: Gross Margin %
metrics.definition: Share of revenue left after cost of goods sold.
metrics.grain: Line-item or order grain; sum revenue and COGS at the SAME grain before dividing. Do NOT average per-row margin percentages (revenue-weighting matters).
anti_patterns: AVG(line_margin_pct) is unweighted — a $1 line and a $1,000 line count equally. Aggregate revenue and cogs, then divide.
Mixing pre-discount price with post-discount revenue inflates margin.
metrics.sane_range: percent 0..100 (negative only on loss-leaders / data error).

metrics.name: Inventory Turnover
metrics.definition: How many times inventory is sold and replaced over a period.
metrics.formula: SUM(units_sold) / NULLIF(AVG(units_on_hand), 0)  -- or COGS / average inventory value
metrics.grain: Per SKU then aggregate; average the stock level over the period (snapshots), not a single point. A SKU with zero average stock yields NULL/undefined turnover, not zero.
anti_patterns: Dividing by a single end-of-period snapshot overstates turnover for seasonal SKUs.
Treating NULL average-stock as 0 turnover hides slow-movers; surface them as 'no stock data / overstock' instead.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..∞ (typical retail 2–12/yr; >50 suggests a grain error).

metrics.name: Return / Refund Rate
metrics.definition: Share of orders (or units) that are returned/refunded.
metrics.grain: Order grain. Denominator must be orders ELIGIBLE to be returned (e.g. delivered), not all rows. Match numerator and denominator population.
anti_patterns: Numerator = refunds but denominator = all orders including undelivered → a 100%-looking rate or a meaningless ratio.
Counting refund rows (a refund can have multiple lines) instead of distinct refunded orders inflates the numerator.

metrics.name: Repeat Purchase Rate
metrics.definition: Share of customers who place more than one order.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id WITH order_count > 1) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0)
metrics.grain: Customer grain. Aggregate orders per customer FIRST (subquery), then count those with >1.
anti_patterns: COUNT(orders) / COUNT(customers) is orders-per-customer, NOT repeat rate.
Window over a join without pre-aggregating customers double-counts.

metrics.name: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)
metrics.definition: Marketing/sales spend divided by newly acquired customers.
metrics.grain: Spend and new-customers must cover the SAME period and ideally the same channel. 'New' = first-order customers in the window.
anti_patterns: Dividing total spend by ALL customers (not just newly acquired) understates CAC.
Attributing all spend to one channel when spend spans channels.

metrics.name: ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) by Channel
metrics.definition: Attributed revenue per dollar of ad spend, by channel.
metrics.grain: Channel grain. Use a consistent attribution weight; revenue and spend at the same channel+period grain.
anti_patterns: Summing fractional attribution weights as if whole orders double-counts revenue.
Per-unit revenue divided by total spend yields meaningless ~0 values (e.g. 5.69e-11) — align magnitudes.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..∞ (typical 1–10x); near-zero scientific-notation = a units/grain error.
